title: Dog Ingested Laundry Detergent Pod: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog swallowed a laundry pod, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn the immediate steps to take and why you must contact a vet now.
content_html:
TL;DR: Laundry detergent pod ingestion is a high-priority medical emergency that can cause chemical burns and life-threatening respiratory distress. Contact a veterinarian or animal poison control immediately if your pet bites or swallows a pod.

What happens if my dog bites or swallows a laundry detergent pod?

  • Laundry detergent pods contain highly concentrated surfactants and chemicals designed to break down tough stains.
  • When a dog bites into a pod, the pressure causes the concentrated liquid to burst.
  • This can lead to chemical burns in the mouth, esophagus, and stomach.
  • More dangerously, the liquid can be inhaled into the lungs (aspiration), causing severe respiratory distress.

Is laundry pod ingestion a veterinary emergency?

  • Yes, the urgency level for laundry pod ingestion is High.
  • Unlike standard liquid detergent, the concentrated nature of pods makes them significantly more toxic.
  • Common risks include airway obstruction from foaming, chemical pneumonia, and severe gastrointestinal ulceration.
  • You should contact a veterinarian or an animal poison control center immediately.

How does a photo of the laundry detergent packaging help my veterinarian?

  • If possible, safely take a quick photo of the product packaging or the specific brand of pod.
  • Different brands use varying concentrations of chemicals.
  • Providing this image to your veterinarian allows them to identify the exact ingredients.
  • This helps the clinic prepare the necessary treatments or neutralizing agents before you arrive at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Following ingestion of laundry detergent pods by dogs, clinical findings commonly include nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. Secondary dehydration and electrolyte imbalance may rarely develop due to protracted vomiting or diarrhea. Mild ocular irritation, with lacrimation and blepharospasm, is possible. Treatment involves dilution with milk or water, which may reduce the risk of spontaneous vomiting. Vomiting is usually self-limiting and responds to short periods of food and water restriction. In severe cases or in animals with sensitive stomachs, antiemetics may be required (e.g., metoclopramide, 0.2-0.4 mg/kg, PO, SC, or IM, qid). Rarely, parenteral fluid therapy may be required. For respiratory exposures, move the animal to an area with fresh air and monitor for dyspnea; stabilization of severely dyspneic animals is essential, and pulmonary edema should be treated as needed. For dermal exposures, bathing with mild shampoo and thorough rinsing is recommended. Ocular exposures should be treated with 10-20 minutes of ocular irrigation with physiologic saline, followed by fluorescein staining of the cornea to detect corneal injury.

Chapter: Toxicology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3004)

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: No. You should never induce vomiting if your dog ingested a detergent pod unless specifically instructed by a veterinarian. The detergent foams easily and can be inhaled into the lungs during vomiting, leading to life-threatening aspiration pneumonia.

faqs.question: What are the immediate signs of detergent poisoning?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include excessive drooling, gagging, persistent coughing, vomiting, and lethargy. If your dog is struggling to breathe or has a bluish tint to their gums, seek emergency care immediately.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog water or milk?
faqs.answer: A small amount of water or milk may help rinse the mouth, but do not force liquids if your dog is coughing or struggling to breathe, as this can worsen respiratory issues.
